subject: Physics
q: An electric bulb is rated as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. What will be the peak magnetic field at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> distance produced by the radiations coming from this bulb? Consider this bulb as a point source with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> efficiency.
question: An electric bulb is rated as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. What will be the peak magnetic field at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> distance produced by the radiations coming from this bulb? Consider this bulb as a point source with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> efficiency.
solution:
Intensity of light is I=PowerArea=200×3.51004π42=0.034 W m-2

Using the relation between magnetic field and intensity, I=B02c2μ0

We have, magnetic field as B0=I×2μ0c=0.034×2×4π×10-73×108=1.69×10-8 T≈1.71×10-8 T

solution:
E=BC=6. (Direction of the wave) ‖(E→×B→)⇒i^=j^×k^
⇒E→=6j^ V m-1

subject: Physics
q: In the given electromagnetic wave $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{y}}=600 \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{kx}) \mathrm{Vm}^{-1}$, intensity of the associated light beam is (in $\mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^2$ : (Given $\epsilon_0=9 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
question: In the given electromagnetic wave $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{y}}=600 \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{kx}) \mathrm{Vm}^{-1}$, intensity of the associated light beam is (in $\mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^2$ : (Given $\epsilon_0=9 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Intensity } & =\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{E}_0^2 \mathrm{c} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-12} \times(600)^2 \times 3 \times 10^8 \\ & =\frac{9}{2} \times 36 \times 3=486 \mathrm{w} / \mathrm{m}^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
The electric field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by :
$E_{y}=69 \sin \left[0.6 \times 10^{3} x-1.8 \times 10^{11} t\right] \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$.
The expression for magnetic field associated with this electromagnetic wave is $\_\_\_\_$ T.
question:
The electric field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by :
$E_{y}=69 \sin \left[0.6 \times 10^{3} x-1.8 \times 10^{11} t\right] \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$.
The expression for magnetic field associated with this electromagnetic wave is $\_\_\_\_$ T.
solution:
Given $E_y = 69 \sin[0.6 \times 10^3 x - 1.8 \times 10^{11} t]$ V/m
Wave propagates along +x (form $kx - \omega t$), $\vec{E}$ is along y.
For EM wave: $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$ = direction of propagation
$\hat{j} \times \hat{k} = \hat{i}$, so $\vec{B}$ is along z-direction.
$B_0 = \frac{E_0}{c} = \frac{69}{3 \times 10^8} = 2.3 \times 10^{-7}$ T
E and B are in phase for plane EM wave.
$B_z = 2.3 \times 10^{-7} \sin[0.6 \times 10^3 x - 1.8 \times 10^{11} t]$ T

subject: Physics
q: A monochromatic beam of light has a frequency <math><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math> and is propagating along the direction <math><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac></math> . It is polarized along the <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> direction. The acceptance form the magnetic field is:
question: A monochromatic beam of light has a frequency <math><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math> and is propagating along the direction <math><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi> </mi></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac></math> . It is polarized along the <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> direction. The acceptance form the magnetic field is:
solution:
E^×B^ should have given direction of wave propagation,⇒  K^×B^∥ i^+j^ 2 
 K^×i^+j^ 2=j^--i^2=i^+j^ 2 ∥i^+j^ 2  . 

Wave propagation vector K^ should be along i^+j^ 2.

q:
The unit of $\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{I}}{\epsilon_0 c}}$ is :
(I = intensity of an electromagnetic wave, $\mathrm{c}:$ speed of light)
question:
The unit of $\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{I}}{\epsilon_0 c}}$ is :
(I = intensity of an electromagnetic wave, $\mathrm{c}:$ speed of light)
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 \mathrm{E}_0^2 \times \mathrm{C} \\ & \mathrm{E}_0=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{I}}{\epsilon_0 \mathrm{C}}}
\end{aligned}$
$E_0$ : electric field N/C

question: Two sources of light emit X-rays of wavelength 1 nm and visible light of wavelength 500 nm, respectively. Both the sources emit light of the same power 200 W. The ratio of the number density of photons of X-rays to the number density of photons of the visible light of the given wavelengths is:
solution:
Ps-Power of source

Ps=nhcλ ; n =  no. of photons emitted/s

⇒  n∝λ  ⇒  n2n1=1/500

q: An electron beam is accelerated by a potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> to hit a metallic target to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>rays. It produces continuous as well as characteristic <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>rays. If <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></math> is the smallest possible wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>ray in the spectrum, the variation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></mfenced></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> is correctly represented in :
question: An electron beam is accelerated by a potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> to hit a metallic target to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>rays. It produces continuous as well as characteristic <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>rays. If <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></math> is the smallest possible wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>ray in the spectrum, the variation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></mfenced></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> is correctly represented in :
solution:
K.E.=eV,

KE=The kinetic energy of electron

The maximum energy of photon=K.E.

eV=hCλmin

⇒λmin V=hCe=constant

Taken log in both sides

lnλmin+lnV=lnconstant

⇒lnλmin=-lnV+ln(constant)

y=-ax+c the straight line of negative slope.

E0=200

I=12ε0E02c
Radiation pressure

subject: Physics
q: A radio transmitter transmits at $830 \mathrm{kHz}$. At a certain distance from the transmitter magnetic field has amplitude $4.82 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~T}$. The electric field and the wavelength are respectively
question: A radio transmitter transmits at $830 \mathrm{kHz}$. At a certain distance from the transmitter magnetic field has amplitude $4.82 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~T}$. The electric field and the wavelength are respectively
solution:
Frequency of EM wave $v=830 \mathrm{KHz}$ $=830 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}$.
Magnetic field, $B=4.82 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~T}$
As we know, frequency, $v=\frac{c}{\lambda}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { or } \lambda & =\frac{c}{v}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{830 \times 10^3} \\
\lambda & \simeq 360 \mathrm{~m} \\
\text { And, } E & =B C=4.82 \times 10^{-11} \times 3 \times 10^8 \\
& =0.014 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A plane electromagnetic wave is moving in free space with velocity $c=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and its electric field is given as $\vec{E}=54 \sin (k z-\omega t) \hat{j} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$, where $\hat{j}$ is the unit vector along $y$-axis. The magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ of the wave is:
question: A plane electromagnetic wave is moving in free space with velocity $c=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and its electric field is given as $\vec{E}=54 \sin (k z-\omega t) \hat{j} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$, where $\hat{j}$ is the unit vector along $y$-axis. The magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ of the wave is:
solution:
For an electromagnetic wave, the relationship between electric and magnetic field amplitudes is $B_0 = \frac{E_0}{c}$.
Here $E_0 = 54$ V/m and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
So, $B_0 = \frac{54}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.8 \times 10^{-7}$ T.
The electric field is along the $\hat{j}$ direction.
Since the wave travels in the $+z$ direction, the magnetic field direction is determined by $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$ pointing in the $+z$ direction.
Using the right-hand rule: $\hat{j} \times (-\hat{i}) = \hat{k}$ (positive $z$ direction), confirming the magnetic field is in the $-\hat{i}$ direction.
Therefore, $\vec{B} = -1.8 \times 10^{-7}\sin(kz - \omega t)\hat{i}$ T.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I: A time varying electric field is a source of changing magnetic field and vice-versa. Thus a disturbance in electric or magnetic field creates EM waves.

Statement II: In a material medium, the EM wave travels with speed v=1μ0ϵ0.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I: A time varying electric field is a source of changing magnetic field and vice-versa. Thus a disturbance in electric or magnetic field creates EM waves.

Statement II: In a material medium, the EM wave travels with speed v=1μ0ϵ0.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both statement I and statement II are true
Both statement I and statement II are false
Statement I is correct but statement II is false
Statement I is incorrect but statement II is true
solution:
According to the Maxwell’s EM theory, the EM waves propagation contains electric and magnetic field vibration in mutually perpendicular direction. Thus, the changing of electric field give rise to magnetic field.

Also, time varying magnetic field produces electric field. According to Faradays law, when magnetic flux changes in the region surrounded by conductor, it produces electric field (induced emf) in conductor. 

In a material medium the EM wave travels with speed given by v=1μεv=1μ0μr(ε0εr)

Thus, statement I is correct but statement II is false.

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave of frequency $1 \times 10^{14}$ hertz is propagating along $\mathrm{z}$-axis. The amplitude of electric field is $4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$. If $\varepsilon_0=8.8 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 / \mathrm{N}-$ $\mathrm{m}^2$, then average energy density of electric field will be:
question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency $1 \times 10^{14}$ hertz is propagating along $\mathrm{z}$-axis. The amplitude of electric field is $4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$. If $\varepsilon_0=8.8 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 / \mathrm{N}-$ $\mathrm{m}^2$, then average energy density of electric field will be:
solution:
Given: Amplitude of electric field,
$$
E_0=4 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{m}
$$
Absolute permitivity,
$$
\varepsilon_0=8.8 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{c}^2 / \mathrm{N}-\mathrm{m}^2
$$
Average energy density $u_E=$ ?
Applying formula,
Average energy density $u_E=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_0 E^2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\Rightarrow \quad u_E=\frac{1}{4} \times 8.8 \times 10^{-12} \times(4)^2 \\
&=35.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The electric field of an electromagnetic wave in free space is represented as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>cos</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>)</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>. The corresponding magnetic induction vector will be :
question: The electric field of an electromagnetic wave in free space is represented as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>cos</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>)</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>. The corresponding magnetic induction vector will be :
solution:
Given E→=E0cos(ωt-kz)i^:

Clearly, (ωt-kz) term represents, electromagnetic wave is travelling along +z direction or along k^.

Now, in vector form, direction of electromagnetive wave is given by C^=E^×B^. Therefore, magnetic field is along j^. 

Also, we know that B0=E0C, where C is speed of light.

Therefore, B→=E0Ccos(ωt-kz)j^.

A beam of light travelling along X-axis is described by the electric field Ey=900sinωt-xc. The ratio of electric force to magnetic force on a charge q moving along Y-axis with a speed of 3×107 m s-1 will be :

[Given speed of light =3×108 m s-1 ]
question:
A beam of light travelling along X-axis is described by the electric field Ey=900sinωt-xc. The ratio of electric force to magnetic force on a charge q moving along Y-axis with a speed of 3×107 m s-1 will be :

[Given speed of light =3×108 m s-1 ]
solution:
Given, Ey=900sinωt-ωxc

Here, E0=900

Electric field and electric force are related as FE=qE0

Magnetic force of a moving charge is given by FB=qvB0

Thus, the ratio FEFB=E0vB0

Using relation between magnetic and electric field, we have

FEFB=cv=3×1083×107=10:1

subject: Physics
q: The frequency of $X$-rays; $\gamma$-rays and ultraviolet rays are respectively $a, b$ and $c$ then
question: The frequency of $X$-rays; $\gamma$-rays and ultraviolet rays are respectively $a, b$ and $c$ then
solution:
Frequency range of $\gamma$-ray, $b=10^{18}-10^{23} \mathrm{~Hz}$
Frequency range of X-ray, $a=10^{16}-10^{20} \mathrm{~Hz}$
Frequency range of ultraviolet ray, $c=10^{15}-10^{17} \mathrm{~Hz}$
$\therefore a < b ; b>c$

subject: Physics
q: A monochromatic source of light operating at $15$ kW emits $2.5\times 10^{22}$ photons/s. The region of an electromagnetic spectrum to which the emitted electromagnetic radiation belongs to ________. (Take $h=6.6\times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c=3\times 10^8$ m/s).
question: A monochromatic source of light operating at $15$ kW emits $2.5\times 10^{22}$ photons/s. The region of an electromagnetic spectrum to which the emitted electromagnetic radiation belongs to ________. (Take $h=6.6\times 10^{-34}$ J·s and $c=3\times 10^8$ m/s).
solution:
Power of the source, $P = 15 \text{ kW} = 15 \times 10^3 \text{ J/s}$

Number of photons emitted per second, $n = 2.5 \times 10^{22} \text{ s}^{-1}$

Energy of one photon, $E = \dfrac{P}{n} = \dfrac{15 \times 10^3}{2.5 \times 10^{22}} = 6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Using $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$, we get $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 330 \text{ nm}$

The wavelength $330 \text{ nm}$ lies in the ultraviolet region of the electromagnetic spectrum.

Answer: Ultraviolet

question: An electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math>, is travelling in a medium whose relative electric permittivity and relative magnetic permeability both are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Its velocity in this medium is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Given : Frequency of wave f=5GHz

=5×109 Hz

Relative permittivity, ϵr=2 and Relative permeability, μr=2 Since speed of light in a medium is given by,

v=1μ∈=1μrμ0·ϵr∈0

v=1μrϵf1μ0ϵ0=Cμrϵr

Where C is speed of light is vacuum

∴ v=3×1084=30×1072 m s-1

=15×107 m s-1

∴ Ans. is 15

c=E0B0
Electric field is expressed as E0=cB0 , E→ is ⊥ to direction x and direction of B→

The expression of electric field is E→=cB0 sin⁡(kx+wt)k^  V m-1

subject: Physics
q: A point light source emits E.M. waves in free space. A detector, placed at a distance of $L$ m, measures the intensity as $I_o$. The detector is now shifted to another location on the same spherical surface ensuring the angle between original location and new location as $45°$. The measured intensity at new location will be _______.
question: A point light source emits E.M. waves in free space. A detector, placed at a distance of $L$ m, measures the intensity as $I_o$. The detector is now shifted to another location on the same spherical surface ensuring the angle between original location and new location as $45°$. The measured intensity at new location will be _______.
solution:
For a point light source, the intensity of electromagnetic waves at a distance $r$ is given by $I = \dfrac{P}{4\pi r^2}$, where $P$ is the power of the source.

Since the detector is moved to a new location on the same spherical surface, the distance from the source remains $L$.

The emission from a point source is isotropic, meaning the intensity depends only on the radial distance from the source and is independent of the angular position.

Therefore, the intensity at the new location remains unchanged, which is $I_o$.

Answer: $I_o$

subject: Physics
q:
The equation of the electric field of an electromagnetic wave propagating through free space is given by : $E=\sqrt{377} \sin \left(6.27 \times 10^{3} t-2.09 \times 10^{-5} x\right) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{C}$
The average power of the electromagnetic wave is $\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}\right) \mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$
(Take $\sqrt{\frac{\mu_{0}}{\varepsilon_{o}}}=377$ in SI units)
question:
The equation of the electric field of an electromagnetic wave propagating through free space is given by : $E=\sqrt{377} \sin \left(6.27 \times 10^{3} t-2.09 \times 10^{-5} x\right) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{C}$
The average power of the electromagnetic wave is $\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}\right) \mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$
(Take $\sqrt{\frac{\mu_{0}}{\varepsilon_{o}}}=377$ in SI units)
solution:
From the electric field equation, amplitude:
$E_0 = \sqrt{377}$ N/C
Average intensity of EM wave:
$I_{avg} = \frac{E_0^2}{2Z_0}$ where $Z_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 377$ Ω
$I_{avg} = \frac{(\sqrt{377})^2}{2 \times 377} = \frac{377}{2 \times 377} = \frac{1}{2}$ W/m²
Given $I_{avg} = \frac{1}{\alpha}$ W/m², we have $\frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{2}$
Therefore, $\alpha = 2$

solution: Infrared is the least energetic thus having biggest wavelength $(\lambda) \&$ gamma rays are most energetic thus having smallest wavelength $(\lambda)$.

B at 0, 0, πk=B0i^+j^2cosπ

Force on charged particle q1
F1=q1V→1×B→1
=4π0.5ci^×-B0i^+j^2=4πB0c22-k^
Force on charged particle q2

=2π0.5ci^×-B0i^+j^2=2πB0c22-k^⇒F1F2=2

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave of intensity $50 \mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ enters in a medium of refractive index 'n' without any loss. The ratio of the magnitudes of electric fields, and the ratio of the magnitudes of magnetic fields of the wave before and after entering into the medium are respectively, given by :
question: An electromagnetic wave of intensity $50 \mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ enters in a medium of refractive index 'n' without any loss. The ratio of the magnitudes of electric fields, and the ratio of the magnitudes of magnetic fields of the wave before and after entering into the medium are respectively, given by :
solution:
The speed of electromagnetic wave in free space is given by

$\mathrm{C}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \epsilon_{0}}}$

In medium, $\mathbf{v}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{k} \in_{0} \mu_{0}}} \ldots$ (ii)

Dividing equation (i) by(ii), we get $\therefore \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{V}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{n}$

$\frac{1}{2} \epsilon_{0} \mathrm{E}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{C}=$ intensity $=\frac{1}{2} \epsilon_{0} \mathrm{kE}^{2} \mathrm{v}$

$\therefore \mathrm{E}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{kE}^{2} \mathrm{v}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{E_{0}^{2}}{E^{2}}=\frac{k V}{C}=\frac{n^{2}}{n} \Rightarrow \frac{E_{0}}{E}=\sqrt{n}$

similarly

$\frac{\mathrm{B}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{C}}{2 \mu_{0}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} \mathrm{v}}{2 \mu_{0}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{B}_{0}}{\mathrm{~B}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{n}}}$
